title: Threads and processes

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Threads and processes; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: The Python Standard Library; Python 3.14; asyncio, concurrent.futures, threading, multiprocessing, queue, and synchronization documentation; atomic topic: Threads and processes
sources.claim: Python threads share process memory and can overlap compatible waiting work, while processes isolate memory and can use multiple CPU cores at the cost of startup and data serialization.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Python threads share process memory and can overlap compatible waiting work, while processes isolate memory and can use multiple CPU cores at the cost of startup and data serialization.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A bounded thread pool overlaps independent HTTP waits, while a measured CPU transform uses a process pool with coarse tasks. Your goal is to select and justify sequential, asynchronous, threaded or process-based execution for a stated task. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Typing, tests, logging and debugging; Generators and lazy pipelines; Concurrency versus parallelism; Mutability, aliases and copying. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Python threads share process memory and can overlap compatible waiting work, while processes isolate memory and can use multiple CPU cores at the cost of startup and data serialization. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Threads still need synchronization, process arguments/results must be transferable, platform start methods differ, worker pools need shutdown, and small tasks can cost more to coordinate than run sequentially. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Processes share ordinary mutable objects automatically, and threads cannot have races because of the interpreter lock.

includes: Choose threads for compatible waiting work and processes for isolated CPU work while accounting for communication cost.
Select and justify sequential, asynchronous, threaded or process-based execution for a stated task.
excludes: Threads still need synchronization, process arguments/results must be transferable, platform start methods differ, worker pools need shutdown, and small tasks can cost more to coordinate than run sequentially.

outcomes: Select and justify sequential, asynchronous, threaded or process-based execution for a stated task.

examples.title: Threads and processes: direct language case
examples.situation: A bounded thread pool overlaps independent HTTP waits, while a measured CPU transform uses a process pool with coarse tasks.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A bounded thread pool overlaps independent HTTP waits, while a measured CPU transform uses a process pool with coarse tasks.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Threads share mutable state and require coordination; processes use isolated memory and explicit communication, so choose after measuring workload and transfer costs.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Threads share mutable state and require coordination; processes use isolated memory and explicit communication, so choose after measuring workload and transfer costs.

examples.title: Threads and processes: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: The command keeps process-pool creation under a main guard so spawn-based platforms do not recursively create workers.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: The command keeps process-pool creation under a main guard so spawn-based platforms do not recursively create workers.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Threads still need synchronization, process arguments/results must be transferable, platform start methods differ, worker pools need shutdown, and small tasks can cost more to coordinate than run sequentially.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Processes share ordinary mutable objects automatically, and threads cannot have races because of the interpreter lock.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Select and justify sequential, asynchronous, threaded or process-based execution for a stated task.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Processes share ordinary mutable objects automatically, and threads cannot have races because of the interpreter lock.
misconceptions.correction: Threads share mutable state and require coordination; processes use isolated memory and explicit communication, so choose after measuring workload and transfer costs.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Processes share ordinary mutable objects automatically, and threads cannot have races because of the interpreter lock.

analogy.example: a coordination desk that overlaps waiting jobs, assigns CPU work to separate rooms, and protects complete shared transitions can introduce Threads and processes, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: concurrency models, asyncio, threads, processes, cancellation, and safety
limitations: the event loop, GIL, serialization, atomicity, scheduling, and cancellation points must be reasoned about technically
